rawText: On 9 July 2026 NutraIngredients reported a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial published in Nature Medicine in which 90 adults with overweight or obesity completed an eight-week low-energy diet achieving at least 8% weight loss before entering a 24-week maintenance phase, with 80 participants completing; those taking pasteurized Akkermansia muciniphila MucT regained an average of 1.2 kg versus 3.2 kg on placebo, with better preservation of insulin sensitivity (Matsuda Index) and no treatment-related serious adverse events. Dr Anneleen Segers of The Akkermansia Company, which owns the ingredient, said it 'could be beneficial… after GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy', while the article states that studies evaluating this application have yet to be conducted; on gut-brain and IBS claims Segers says 'these are very preliminary data'. · ANGLES: (1) A rare good-news evidence story — this is what a properly controlled supplement trial looks like and it earns its result; use it as the positive benchmark against which weaker weeks are judged. (2) The extension gap — the primary endpoint is evidenced, the GLP-1 aftercare hook is not, and the article says so plainly. (3) Weight regain after coming off a GLP-1 is a live buyer moment for women 35–60; what does the evidence actually cover? · APP CONNECTION: compare claim vs proof — paste the product and the app separates the claim the trial supports (weight-regain maintenance) from the claim it does not (post-GLP-1 use). · SECONDARY: Nature Medicine 32, 2107–2116 (2026), doi 10.1038/s41591-026-04394-7, 'Pasteurized Akkermansia muciniphila MucT for weight loss maintenance in people with overweight and obesity: a controlled randomized trial', Mount, S. et al (grade A — cited in the NI article, not independently fetched) · SOURCE GRADE: B (trade journalism, date-verified 09-Jul-2026), resting on a grade A peer-reviewed trial. · NOTE: the ingredient is already sold to UK consumers.
